results.webTitle: MI5/MI6 torture collusion report published: Politics live blog

fields.headline: MI5/MI6 torture collusion report published: Politics live blog
fields.trailText: Andrew Sparrow's rolling coverage of all the day's political developments as they happen, including the publication of the Gibson inquiry report into allegations that MI5 and MI6 colluded in torture, and Kenneth Clarke's statement about it to parliament
fields.bodyText: • Amnesty International has described the government's response to the Gibson report into British collusion with torture and rendition as "disappointing" and called for a judge-led inquiry. This is from Amnesty International's Europe director John Dalhuisen. Today's announcement about how the UK government now intends to address torture allegations is as disappointing as it is predictable. The interim report of the detainee inquiry has identified 27 issues for further examination, underscoring the need for an effective, independent, human rights-compliant inquiry. Combating torture requires coming clean about when and where it occurred, who was involved and holding those responsible to account. The intelligence and security committee is the wrong body for this vital job and today's announcement is yet another effort by the UK to shirk its responsibilities. Handing the investigation over to the ISC raises the prospect that much of the truth may remain buried, and that no full judge-led inquiry will ever materialise. • David Cameron has pledged to redouble efforts to combat extremism. He was speaking following the conviction of Michael Adebolajo and Michael Adebowale for the murder of the soldier Lee Rigby near a military barracks in London, in the first al-Qaida-inspired attack to claim a life on British soil since 7 July 2005. Cameron said: The whole country was completely shocked by the murder and the whole country united in condemnation at what happened. We have to redouble our efforts to confront the poisonous narrative of extremism and violence and make sure we do everything to beat it. • The Conservartive MP Jacob Rees-Mogg has used an article for the Telegraph to say that the Tories have no chance of winning the general election unless they reach an accord of some kind with Ukip. Here's an extract. Ukip’s success is making it extremely difficult for the Conservatives to win in marginal seats. This risks a strongly pro-European Labour Party having a majority against the combined interests and superiority in terms of votes of the Right. The Survation polls indicate that about a third of Ukip voters self-identify as former Tories and a similar percentage as being willing to leave Ukip to stop Mr Miliband. This is bound to understate the true number as people rarely want to appear indecisive in their voting choice. Conservatives and Ukip agree on a wide range of policy areas and there is a growing realisation that the European Union and the European Court of Human Rights are key to a domestic political agenda. The electorate wants decision-making returned to Westminster and a combination between the two parties of the Right could be sufficient to make it happen. With a division it cannot. That's all from me for today. I've got a meeting to go to. Once again, I hope you all have a Happy Christmas. Back to the Gibson report. Libyan politician Abdelhakim Belhadj, who has accused Britain of complicity in his rendition to the regime of ousted dictator Muammar Gaddafi, said the World at One that an independent inquiry into the rendition allegations remained important. It is really central to our efforts to uphold human rights in this country and abroad. We don't want what happened to us to happen to others. It's one of the most important issues concerning the future of Libya and its human rights. I'm not finishing for the day yet, but I'd like to take a moment to wish all readers a Happy Christmas, and to thank those you who have been passing on season's greetings BTL. I deeply appreciate, well, not quite all the comments posted on my blog, but certainly the vast majority of them, and the comments, links and information posted here, as well as the quality of the conversation (generally) adds immeasurably to the quality of the blog. I hope you all have a very good break. Except you don't need to have a break. I will try to ensure that we have a readers' edition running on most weekdays during the holiday period. I will be back blogging again regularly from Monday 6 January. • An official inquiry has found that British agents may have been "inappropriately involved" in the rendition and ill-treatment of terrorist suspects. In a statement to the Commons, Kenneth Clarke, the minister without portfolio, said that the 27 questions raised by the inquiry by Sir Peter Gibson, which never got the chance to finish its work because criminal proceedings are underway, would be investigated by parliament's intelligence and security committee. Gibson said: It does appear from the documents that the United Kingdom may have been inappropriately involved in some renditions. That is a very serious matter. And no doubt any future inquiry would want to look at that. • Nick Clegg has indicated that he may be prepared to drop his party's opposition to the expansion of airport capacity in the south-east of England. • Vince Cable has said that London is London "is becoming a giant suction machine draining the life out of the rest of the country". He made the point as he was explaining why he thought regional airports should be expanded, not Heathrow. • Ed Balls has asked all shadow cabinet members to set out initial ideas for money-saving public service reforms by the spring in what is being billed as a "zero-based spending review" due to be completed in government. • General Sir Nicholas Houghton, head of the armed forces has said that Britain is in danger of being left with hollowed-out armed forces. In a speech, he said that although the armed forces had "exquisite" equipment, training levels were being squeezed and manpower was increasingly seen as "an overhead". • Chris Grayling, the justice secretary, has revealed that the private outsourcing firm Serco has agreed to repay £68.5m plus VAT in overcharging on its contract to provide electronic tagging of offenders. • Sir Bernard Hogan-Howe, the Metropolitan police commissioner, has admitted there is cause for concern over the accuracy of crime statistics. In an interview on the Today programme, he insisted allegations that figures were being fiddled to make them look better were being investigated. • David Cameron has used a visit to Flanders to announce that the government will provide £5m to help fund war memorials. Here's a statement from the intelligence and security committee about the Gibson report. The Detainee Inquiry, headed by Sir Peter Gibson, has today published its report summarising the preparatory work of the Inquiry and highlighting the themes and issues which the Inquiry believes should be subject to further examination. The Intelligence and Security Committee of Parliament welcomes the publication of this interim report. The report raises important questions as to the UK Agencies’ and Government’s policies, procedures and conduct in relation to detainees in the decade following the terrorist attacks of 11 September 2001. The Prime Minister has asked the Committee to consider those issues identified by Sir Peter Gibson. The Committee has agreed to undertake this responsibility. In previous Parliaments, the ISC has published reports on detainees and rendition, and submitted reports to the then Prime Minister on allegations of complicity in mistreatment of detainees, and on the Government’s draft consolidated guidance on detainees. Those previous Reports were incomplete and, in some important respects, unsatisfactory since, after they had been published, new information which had not been made available to the Committee became known. At that time the intelligence Agencies were not under any statutory obligation to provide the Committee with all relevant information in their possession. They were entitled to reach their own decision as to what to provide. Since those reports were produced, the ISC has been given important new powers under the Justice and Security Act. The Agencies are now under a statutory obligation to provide the Committee with all relevant material. The Committee now has a statutory right to consider all aspects of work across the intelligence community, which is backed by the power for its staff to go into the Agencies themselves and inspect their files. These powers are already being used and will be available for all the Committee’s future work. The Committee is, therefore, satisfied that the problems that occurred in the past will not, and cannot, recur. The Committee is currently focussed on its detailed investigation into the intelligence and security Agencies’ actions in relation to those charged with the murder of Drummer Lee Rigby in Woolwich. The Committee will also be undertaking a major inquiry into the balance between privacy and security. In order to ensure that the work arising from the Gibson Inquiry receives the level of scrutiny required, the Committee has therefore requested additional resources commensurate with the task and received assurances that those will be provided. The Committee will also be given access to all of the information (including to primary material) that Government departments and Agencies had provided to the Gibson Inquiry. Once the Committee receives the Agencies’ written responses to the 27 questions identified by Sir Peter and his panel, it will commence its investigation. And here is some more Twitter comment on the Gibson report, and the Commons statement about it. From the Daily Mail's Tim Shipman From Reprieve UK From the Guardian's Tom Clark From Lord Strasburger, a Lib Dem peer From Labour's Sadiq Khan From ITV's Lucy Manning Peter Riddell, the former Times journalist who was a member of the Gibson inquiry until he left to take up a post as director of the Institute for Government, says questions remain unanswered. The Commons statement is now over. Here's the Guardian's story about the Gibson report. And here's how it starts. Dozens of questions about the UK's involvement in the rendition andtorture of terrorism suspects after 9/11 remain unanswered, an official inquiry has concluded. In his interim report, the inquiry head Sir Peter Gibson raised 27 questions to be asked of the involvement of government ministers and officers from MI5 and MI6 in the mistreatment of detainees in the so-called war on terror. Gibson concluded that the questions concern four broad themes: interrogation; rendition; the training of intelligence officers and the guidance they received; and communications between the intelligence agencies and government. He also highlighted the rendition of two Libyan nationals who were taken to Tripoli against their will, along with their families, in 2004. "There are serious allegations of UK involvement in rendition in relation to the two Libya nationals. These plainly required investigation," he said. Other questions that Gibson said demanded answers included "whether there was an apparent willingness, at least at some levels within the agencies, to condone, encourage or take advantage of a rendition operation". He also questioned "whether government and agencies became inappropriately involved in such renditions". Here are some extracts from Kenneth Clarke's statement On the broad findings of the Gibson report It does identify 27 issues which do require further examination. These are grouped under four broad themes, relating to interrogation and treatment, renditions, guidance and training, and matters relating to policy and communications. The period of time was one in which we and our international partners were suddenly adapting to a completely new scale and type of threat from fundamentalist, religious extremists. Many UK intelligence officers had to operate in extraordinarily challenging environments subject to real personal danger. But everyone in the government and everyone in the agencies accept this bravery has to be combined with clear rules of proportionality, accountability, to ensure we uphold the values we are working hard to defend. While we accept intelligence operations must be conducted in the strictest secrecy, we also expect there to be strict oversight of those operations to ensure at all times they respect the human rights that are a cornerstone of this country's values. On British agents not being "directly responsible" for the mistreatment of detainees [The report] finds no evidence in the documents to support any allegation UK intelligence officers were directly responsible for the mistreatment of detainees held by other countries overseas. No material has been referred by the panel to the police for further consideration. On the specific failings identified in the report It is now clear that our agencies and their staff were in some respects not prepared for the extreme demands suddenly placed upon them. The guidance regulating how intelligence officers should act was inadequate. The practices of some of our international partners should have been understood much sooner. Oversight was not robust enough and there was no mechanism in the civil courts for allegations against the security and intelligence services to be examined properly. Most of these problems related to a relatively short period of time in the early 2000s but there is some damage to our reputation, which prides itself as a beacon of justice, human rights and the rule of law. On the government's "regret" I believe I speak for the whole House, on all sides, when I say if failures and mistakes were made in this period that is a matter of sincere regret. On the new ISC inquiry It would be wrong to leave these issues, many of which relate to matters of policy, unexamined for the unknown amount of time it will take for the police to complete their related investigations ... The prime minister has discussed and agreed with the intelligence and security committee (ISC) of parliament they will inquire into the themes and issues which Sir Peter has raised. They will take further evidence and they will report to the government and to parliament on the outcome of their inquiry. Andrew Mitchell, the Conservative former chief whip, says Jack Straw was dealing with quite extraordinary circumstances after 9/11. Straw acted with "complete responsibility" and "total integrity". Clarke endorses that. He says there is nothing in the report that casts doubt on Straw's integrity. Here's an extract from the statement Sadiq Khan, the shadow justice secretary, gave when he was responding to Kenneth Clarke earlier. Notwithstanding the crucial work these agencies do in keeping us safe, any allegations of involvement by members of our security and intelligence services in serious breaches of the law need proper and full investigation. Any acts that might contravene the law in the ways alleged would run counter to everything our nation stands for and believes in. It’s for this reason it’s important there is a full and proper investigation with any wrongdoing being exposed and those responsible brought to account. We also need to ensure the appropriate lessons are learnt and that there is no repeat in the future. And we need to do this in as open and transparent a manner as possible, in a way that maintains the confidence of the public. Back in the Commons Richard Ottoway, the Conservative chair of the Commons foreign affairs committee, asks if Clarke will have discussions with the human rights groups. Clarke says he has the greatest sympathy with human rights groups. They are "on the side of the angels". They are backing ideas he supports. But they are being "wholly unrealistic" if they think it is right for the workings of the intelligence agencies to be discussed in open court, he says. Here is some reaction to the report from theKeith Best, chief executive of the Freedom from Torture campaign. Today’s announcement hints at a spectacular u-turn on this Government’s repeated commitment to hold an independent, judge-led inquiry to get to the bottom of unresolved allegations that the UK was complicit in torture in the aftermath of 9/11. The government has sat on the findings of Sir Peter Gibson’s aborted inquiry for 18 months. This time could have been used to design a new human rights-compliant inquiry that would be credible both in the eyes of those who were subjected to torture and the British public. However, it appears that the Government has been busy trying to concoct an even less transparent and independent process. Unless Mr Cameron delivers the judge-led inquiry he promised, he will lose the moral high ground he sought to assert when he first came to power and announced his intention to draw a line under this appalling episode. Instead he will face accusations that he has slowly but surely become part of the whitewash. Those who suffer torture have a right to redress. And we know from our work with thousands of torture survivors that seeing justice done enables them to move on with their lives. We joined the boycott of Sir Peter’s Gibson inquiry precisely because this group of survivors was thrust to the sidelines; they have been in limbo ever since. Mr Clarke’s ambiguous announcement today suggests they will stay there. My colleague Richard Norton-Taylor has sent another note about the Clarke statement. Clarke insists the ISC was "fully independent of government". But it still relies on the cooperation of the heads of MI5, MI6, and GCHQ and still meets in private, with its members approved by the PM. And its resources are still inadequate - as its members have admitted. Andrew Tyrie, a Conservative MP and a campaigner on rendition issues, says the intelligence services do a good job. But this statement will not help them. There should be a full independent inquiry, he says. How can people trust the ISC when it said in an earlier report - wrongly - that British officers were not involved in rendition. Clarke says he shares Tyrie's concerns about the idea of British officials being involved in rendition. But an inquiry could not take evidence alongside an ongoing police investigation, he says. So do we do nothing? Or do we get the ISC to investigate? Clarke says he is sorry Tyrie is dismissive of the ISC's powers. But the ISC now has "far more powers than it previously had". Last time the ISC was not given information assembled by the agencies for their own use. It is highly unlikely that would happen again. Jack Straw,the Labour foreign secretary at the time of 9/11, is speaking now. He says he welcomes the fact that there will be an ISC inquiry. Straw says he acted at all times as foreign secretary in accordance with his legal duties and international law. At no time was he complicit in rendition by the US or any other state. But he did agree that the UK would not stop British nationals being transferred to Guantanamo Bay. He did so after legal advice, and knowing that the alternative was their continued detention in Afghanistan. But he did not agree to them being mistreated, he says. And the British government made repeated complaints to the Americans about the way they were being treated, he says. He reminds MPs that this happened in extraordinary circumstances - after the 9/11 attacks. The bravery of the agencies helped to keep people safe, he says. Clarke says he has considerable respect for Straw. And he has sympathy for the position he finds himself in, where it has been difficult for him to protect his reputation. Back in the Commons, Sir Malcolm Rifkind, chair of the ISC, is speaking now. The ISC has already published reports on the treatment of detainees. But they were "incomplete" and "unsatisfactory" because the agencies did not provide all the information need. Now the committee has the power to go into the agencies and demand to see files. Because of that, and because it is getting extra resources, the ISC is happy to carry out the inquiry that David Cameron wants, Rifkind says. And this is what it says about training. The adequacy of training and guidance for UK personnel is an issue relevant to many of the matters considered by the Inquiry. The Inquiry has not identified significant grounds to doubt that the overall tenor of the instruction to personnel was that detainees must be treated humanely and consistently with the UK’s international legal obligations. But officers deployed on the ground, especially to an area of military operations, need clear guidance on when and with whom to raise concerns, mindful of the sensitivities involved in multi- national military and intelligence operations. The question of sharing with liaison partners intelligence relating to individuals who are or may be detained raises questions of moral and legal complexity that are also sensitive and difficult. There is an issue whether, from 2001 – 2004, guidance may have been slow to develop, and may have been too limited, being reactive to individual events and lacking in detail. The combined effect of the ISC’s investigations and detainee issues becoming high profile in the spring of 2004 led to formal written guidance in the summer of 2004. This borrowed from military guidance and identified specific forms of unacceptable treatment. From 2004 onwards, the guidance developed and in stages improved in its clarity, detail and the range of issues it covered. The question whether these improvements may have been slower to develop than might legitimately have been expected warrants investigation. It was not until 2010 that guidance addressing both liaison relationships and detainee interviewing was consolidated. Also there are questions as to whether support for rendition operations was adequately addressed in the guidance and, prior to 2010, whether the guidance dealt sufficiently with all the factors relevant to the legality of detention abroad. And this is what it says about collusion in rendition. Rendition may be regarded as a form of mistreatment, as the Inquiry’s terms of reference recognise. The US’s rendition programme expanded rapidly and changed in nature in the aftermath of 9/11. The Inquiry would have wished to examine when the Government came to understand the scope of the US policy and whether the Government and its Agencies responded adequately once they became aware of renditions or proposed renditions of British nationals and UK residents. There is an issue as to whether the Government and the Agencies may have become inappropriately involved in some cases of rendition, and whether an adequate policy was formulated and guidance issued to personnel, addressing the extent to which it was proper for the UK to support or assist renditions carried out by other countries. This is what the report says in its summary about collusion in the torture of detainees. The documents received by the Inquiry indicate that in some instances UK intelligence officers were aware of inappropriate interrogation techniques and mistreatment or allegations of mistreatment of some detainees by liaison partners from other countries. Many of these instances were reported to Agency Head Offices. The Inquiry would have wished to examine whether that reporting was adequate and, in particular, whether the Agency Head Offices then responded adequately or, in some cases, at all. There is an issue as to whether the Agencies raised allegations of mistreatment of detainees with liaison partners with sufficient vigour, and as to the adequacy of assurances sought by the Agencies. In some cases, documents indicate that the Agencies continued to engage with liaison partners in relation to individual detainees where treatment issues may have justified withdrawal or the seeking of appropriate assurances. The Inquiry would have wished to investigate whether the legality of the detainees’ detention abroad and the Agencies’ own methods of questioning were subject to sufficient scrutiny and consideration. Here's the Gibson report (pdf). Some journalists have had a chance to read the report under embargo. Here are some of their tweets. My colleague Richard Norton-Taylor has sent me a quick note about Kenneth Clarke's statement. Ken Clarke choses key words carefully: there is no evidence to support allegations that the UK was "directly responsible" for the treatment of detained terror suspects and reforms needed, because of damage to Britain's "reputation" - though he admits some "failures and mistakes" . Ken Clarke's statement is finished. I can now post the text of a news release sent out by the Gibson inquiry earlier under embargo. Here it is. Today the Government has published a report submitted to the Prime Minister by Sir Peter Gibson and Dame Janet Paraskeva, the Panel of the Detainee Inquiry, on the Inquiry’s work. The Inquiry’s Report speaks for itself. It is a rigorous, thorough and independent piece of work. It reveals more information than ever before about the workings of Government and the Agencies, on the issues highlighted in the report. Sir Peter said: “There are matters which deserve further investigation. That is what the documents have disclosed and we explain why in our report.” Dame Janet said: “We have worked hard to put as much as possible into the public domain. I do hope the Government will decide to build on our work in a future Inquiry and give the detainees a chance to have their say.” The library of documents, the analysis of information and preliminary identification of potential witnesses the Inquiry carried out, will save any subsequent Inquiry a huge amount of time and resource. The report does not find facts or reach conclusions. It is based on the scrutiny of documents, no witness has yet had the opportunity to explain or add to this information. But the Inquiry has shone a bright light onto issues which might be investigated further by a future Inquiry or on which the Government can take action now. The Inquiry covered four separate themes: interrogation and treatment issues, rendition, training and guidance as well as policy and communications. Its work revealed 27 separate issues the Inquiry would like to have investigated further and which might be followed up by a future Inquiry. In summary the report says: Interrogation and Treatment issues: Documents indicate that in some instances UK intelligence officers were aware of inappropriate interrogation techniques and mistreatment or allegations of mistreatment of some detainees by liaison partners from other countries. Rendition Documents indicate that Government or its Agencies may have become inappropriately involved in some cases of rendition. Training and Guidance No reason to doubt that instruction to personnel was that detainees must be treated humanely and consistently with UK’s international legal obligations. But officers on the ground needed clear guidance on when and with whom to raise concerns. Policy and Communications Documents raise the question whether the Agencies could have identified possible patterns of detainee mistreatment more quickly and whether or not sufficient information was given to the ISC to enable it to perform its duties. Clarke says, as a result of these changes, a line has been drawn under the past. David Cameron has agreed that the intelligence and security committee (ISC) will look at the themes and issues that the Gibson report raises. The committee will get extra resources, so that it can undertake this work without undermining its inquiries into the Woolwich killing and privacy and security. The agencies are being asked to respond to the claims made about them in the Gibson report in a submission to the ISC. Once the ISC work is over, the government will consider whether a full judicial inquiry into these matters is needed. Clarke says the security agencies now operate under much clearer guidance. This covers how detainees should be monitored. It is scrutinised by the intelligence services commissioner. Ministers must be told about any risk to detainees, he says. The intelligence agencies are accountable to the intelligence and security committee. It has been given stronger powers, Clarke says. Clarke says his "secret courts" legislation (my phrase, not his) now means some of these issues can be addressed in a court of law. Clarke says most of these problems relate to a relatively short period of time. But there has been reputational damage to the UK, he says. This is a matter of regret. Clarke says British officials were working in difficult circumstances at the time. They worked in dangerous conditions. But they should also be expected to operate under a clear framework of values. Clarke says the report presents a picture of agencies struggling to cope with a new environment. In some respects, their preparation was not adequate. Clarke says the police launched a criminal investigation into rendition to Libya. This raised the prospect of indefinite delay before the Gibson inquiry could start taking evidence. As a result, it was decided that the Gibson inquiry would present an interim report, based on the findings of their preliminary research. It is a substantial report, Clarke says. The panel looked at 20,000 documents. They found no evidence of torture by British officials. But there were 27 cases meriting further investigation. Ken Clarke is making his statement now. He says Sir Peter Gibson's inquiry was set up in 2010. After 9/11 there were allegations that British officers were aware of detainees being treated improperly. When he set up the inquiry, David Cameron said there was no evidence of British officials actually being involved in torture themselves. Cameron said he wanted to settle the civil compensation claims submitted by British detainees sent to Guantanamo Bay. A significant settlement was agreed. The Gibson inquiry was set up. John Bercow, the Speaker, says some MPs will be named personally in the statement. Jack Straw is one. He will be given extra time to ask a question, Bercow says. Today's Daily Mail says the Gibson report will say that British spies were aware of up to 40 detainees being tortured. We should get the full details any minute now. Kenneth Clarke, the minister without portfolio, is due to give his statement to the Commons about the Gibson report shortly. Here's the Guardian's preview story. Call Clegg has finished. The most interesting thing Nick Clegg had to say was about Heathrow. In their 9.30 news bulletin, LBC interpreted Clegg's remarks as his coming out against a third runway at Heathrow. But LBC got it wrong. What is interesting is that Clegg, and the Lib Dems, are now softening their opposition to a third runway at Heathrow. He said that he has always opposed Heathrow expansion "in the past" on environmental grounds. And he said he still believed that airport expansion in the south east should only take place provided that it does not make noise pollution worse, and that Britain keeps its carbon emission targets. This sounded like opposition to Heathrow. But, actually, the Airports Commission report says that the number of people affected by noise around Heathrow is expected to fall by 2030 (because aircraft are getting quieter) and that the effect of a third runway would be "roughly neutral". It also says that all the runway options it proposes are compatible with Britain meeting its emissions targets. In the past the Lib Dems used to oppose all airport expansion in the south east. Clegg's comment suggest the party could just about accept a new runway at Heathrow, although it would probably much prefer one at Gatwick. Clegg said the party would not take a view until the Airports Commission published its final report in 2015. In a separate interview on the Radio 4 Today programme Vince Cable also indicated that the Lib Dems' opposition to Heathrow was softening. When asked if he still believed that a third runway was not going to happen, as he once said categorically in an interview, he said that he now thought it was "very unlikely". However, Cable also stressed that he was personally very strongly opposed to Heathrow expansion. He is MP for Twickenham and, for him, this is a huge local issue. I have got to go to a meeting. I will post again at about 11.30, when the Gibson statement starts. Q: Is it offensive for two white people to have a conversation and to use the word golliwog? Nick Ferrari says he's raising it because of this legal ruling. Clegg says this is not a word that he would use, but he does not know the case and does not want to comment on the ruling. Q: Do you have any memories of Peter Wright, the headmaster at Caldicott school who has been convicted of child abuse? Yes, says Clegg.Wright was headteacher when he was there. He was a "slightly remote" figure. It is shocking to learn that this abuse was going on, when the school seemed normal to pupils. Q: Are you opposed to grammar schools? Clegg says he is not against grammar schools. Schools funded by the taxpayer should not be able to select children on the basis of ability. If they can do that, they can "cream skim" the brightest children. Q: What should be done about Lords reform? Clegg says the Lords should be elected. But the main parties blocked Lords reform, he says. People should speak to "David Miliband", Clegg says before correcting himself and saying Ed Miliband, and David Cameron about this. Q: Is it true that former MEPs lose their pensions if they criticise the EU? So how can we trust what you say about the EU? Clegg says he has heard plenty of "loopy" theories, but that this one takes the biscuit. He laughs. He can guarantee the caller that his pension is not affected by what he says about the EU. Q: What do you think about the plan for an England/Afghanistan football match at Wembley? Clegg says the Afghans are incredibly proud people. Their football team should be acknowledged. Q: But isn't it an odd idea? Cameron visiting the troops, and saying here's Michael Owen, let's have a game of football. It's nuts? Clegg asks what's wrong with having a football match with Afghanistan. Q: What do you think about David Cameron saying the mission has been accomplished in Afghanistan? Clegg says Cameron did not say mission accomplished. He said that, when the British troops left, they would be able to leave with their heads held high. No one is pretending Afghanistan will be live Switzerland. But Cameron was making the point that British soldiers performed the job they were asked to to. Q: So it is mission accomplished? Of course, for the military mission. But Cameron was not claiming Afghanistan is problem free. That is not what he said, and not what he thinks. Q: Boris Johnson thinks you are a "lapdog of David Cameron" and a "yellow albatross" around the neck of the coalition. Clegg says Johnson is upset that Clegg disagrees with his "patronising" remarks about IQ. Q: Why do you not appreciated the economic case for expanding Heathrow? I live under the flightpath and favour expansion, because it would be good for local jobs? Clegg says he has always opposed Heathrow expansion in the past on environmental grounds. He would not back anything that would increase noise levels, or breach Britain's climate change obligations. (Actually, the Davies report says Heathrow can be expanded without noise levels increasing, and without Britain's climate change obligations being met.) Q: So what do you want? Clegg says he does not want to see aviation expansion take place in the south east of England in a way that would make noise or environmental pollution worse, or that would breach climate change rules. The Davies report says the increase in flights in the future will come from point-to-point flights. It questions the need for a hub airport. He says Boris Island would not remotely meet these tests. Q: What do you think about the plan that the Home Office is reportedly looking at, for a cap of 75,000 on EU immigration? Clegg says that would be really bad for the economy. Q: Why? Clegg says it's about the principle of free movement. Q: But what about people who come to camp in Marble Arch and defecate in the bushes? Come on, says Clegg. We're talking about Germany lawyers, and Swedish engineers. More than 3m people have jobs that depend on the UK being in the EU. They would not benefit if Britain were to pull out. If Britain just pulled up the drawbridge, other countries would too. Q: How many immigrants would you allow into the country? The Commons does not seem able to do anything. Clegg says the government can act. It already imposes immigration controls. And, as Clegg has said before, he would like better exit checks. Migration figures also depend on how many Britons decide to leave the UK, he says. Q: You have no control over who comes to this country. Clegg says that's not true. And he says it is important to point out that the right to come to the UK is not the same as the right to claim benefits. Today the government is publishing the report from Sir Peter Gibson, who led an inquiry set up to investigate allegations that MI5 and MI6 colluded in the torture and rendition of terrorist suspects. Rather, it is publishing Gibson's interim report, because the inquiry was shelved before it had finished its work when a criminal investigation was launched. Ian Cobain, the Guardian's expert on this subject, said last week that the report was "reported to have concluded that British intelligence officers were aware that detainees were being mistreated in prisons across the globe." And today he and Richard Norton-Taylor report that Gibson will call for the inquiry to be continued by the intelligence and security committee. Here's the start of their story. The stalled official inquiry into the UK's involvement in rendition and torture in the years after 9/11 is to be handed to the controversial intelligence and security committee (ISC), the government will announce on Thursday. The decision follows years of assurances by ministers that the inquiry would be headed by a senior judge, with the prime minister having told MPs that no other arrangement would command public confidence. It triggered warnings from human rights groups that the government risked complaints of a cover-up. The ISC is the oversight body that has been condemned for failing to report on the bulk surveillance operations being conducted by the UK's signals intelligence agency, GCHQ, until after they became public. Its inquiry into rendition concluded only that MI5 and MI6 had been slow to spot what the CIA had been doing. Some details about the involvement of the UK's intelligence agencies in the abuse of terrorism suspects may appear in the interim report of the stalled inquiry when it is published on Thursday. The report is being redacted before publication. The report will be published at about 11.30am and Kenneth Clarke, the minister without portfolio, will be making a statement on it to parliament. I'll be covering it live. Before that we've got Nick Clegg's LBC phone-in at 9am. I will be covering that, but I have to go to a meeting this morning and so I'm afraid the blog will be on automatic pilot (ie, it won't get updated) between about 10am and the start of the Clarke statement. If you want to follow me on Twitter, I’m on @AndrewSparrow.

results.webTitle: Politics live: readers' edition - Friday 25 October

fields.headline: Politics live: readers' edition - Friday 25 October
fields.trailText: Share breaking news, leave links to interesting articles online and chat about the day's political events in our open thread
fields.bodyText: I'm not writing my usual Politics Live blog today, but, as an alternative, here's Politics Live: readers' edition. It's intended to be a place where you can catch up with the latest news and find links to good politics blogs and articles on the web. Please feel free to use this as somewhere you can comment on any of the day's political stories - just as you do when I'm writing the daily blog. It would be particularly useful for readers to flag up new material in the comments - breaking news or blogposts or tweets that are worth passing on because someone is going to find them interesting. A lot of what I do on my blog is aggregation - finding the good stuff and passing it on - and you can do this, too (as I know, because it happens every day when I'm blogging). All of today's Guardian politics stories are here, and all the politics stories filed on Thursday including some in today's paper, are here.

results.webTitle: Let's kick uglism out of politics | David Schneider

fields.headline: Let's kick uglism out of politics
fields.trailText: David Schneider: Shallow voters reject Ed Miliband simply because he looks like a platypus. Was Labour's struggle to make themselves electable just about ditching unattractive leaders for one who was reasonably hot?
fields.bodyText: I was appalled to see in a recent opinion poll that 74% of voters would be more likely to vote Labour if Ed Miliband changed his face. Actually, I wasn't appalled because I made that up. But it wouldn't surprise me if Labour's lead hit double figures on condition that Ed swapped the Miliface for someone else's. Possibly his brother David's. I'm sure most politicians would change their face if it would win them an election (most of them have got two anyway), but are voters really that shallow? François Hollande clearly thinks so, putting pressure on French news agencies not to show a picture of him gurning like a hamster who'd overreached himself cheekwise. Similarly, David Cameron can't be happy about the picture of him getting 40 winks before his sister-in-law's wedding – ironic, seeing as his government is planning to cut the number of winks to 32 (except for those earning over £150K. We don't want to drive big winkers out of the country). Everyone from seven-year-olds who look crap on school photo day to Neil Kinnock arse-over-tipping on the beach knows that images can be devastating. Or redeeming. Remember that footage of Gordon Brown leaving Downing Street for the last time, hand-in-hand with his two little kids? I watched that next to a Labour supporter who referred to the boys as "10 seats" and "20 seats", because that's how many marginals he felt could have been won had Brown allowed them to be photographed with him during the campaign. We may try to pretend that it's all about policy, that the next election will be fought over welfare or the NHS, but the truth is it's more likely to be won and lost on whether someone can get a picture of Cameron or Miliband taking a dump. And the Tories might not even need dumpgate. Judging from my highly scientific poll of a group of people I had a row with in Garfunkel's, Labour may be deprived of a majority at the next election simply because some people could never vote for Miliband as he "looks like a human platypus" (at least it's more original than Wallace and Gromit). What do they want? A manifesto that includes guarantees of above-inflation rises in cheekbones and the complete elimination of monobrows by 2020? Is that how things are now? Was Labour's struggle in the 80s and 90s to make themselves electable just about finding a leader who was reasonably hot (if you'll forgive me for making you think of Blair in those terms)? Not so much clause IV as clause phwoar. It's the dental work, stupid, not the economy. Of course, Cameron is no Ryan Gosling when it comes to looks. He can't be much more than a six, even to his wife – even to a Tory shires lady who really hates badgers. Clegg was maybe a seven just before the last election and a three after it. At best. Which all sounds a bit shallow, and yet that's how people seem to be judging Miliband, certainly in the Heathrow Garfunkel's. I suspect that one reason the accusations of weakness and indecision stick to him, even though he has often showed considerable boldness (Murdoch, Syria, the unions, fratricide), is because his body language seems so apologetic (has no one in the Labour party heard of Pilates?). It seems that people who would never dream of not voting for someone because they were black or gay feel it is all right to not vote for someone because they "don't look right". Of course, it's not just politicians whom we judge by their looks. Looks are what count on TV, in the office, even in the bedroom (OK, that's probably allowable). As an actor, I've profited from being "funny-looking". A review described me as "incredibly grotesque, with a face you'd only expect to see on The X-Files". It was meant (and taken) as a compliment. I'm fine with my looks (do I protest too much?), but they have made me aware of how much our faces define us. Look at TV presenters. The Law of Brucie states that men stay attractive until roughly 30 years after their demise (those worms coming out of our skull just make us look "distinguished"), but if Holly Willoughby ever sprouts so much as a frown-line, she'll be bagged up and carted off before you can say "hot flush". This is always framed in terms of sexism and ageism, but seeing as men with a deformity or a birthmark find it as difficult to get presenting gigs as women over 40, perhaps the opposition isn't men/women or young women/old women but attractive/unattractive. Perhaps it's uglism, plain and simple – like the faces of those who are excluded (as you'd say if you were uglist). I'm sure the quite hot Richard Dawkins (a solid seven, I reckon) would say the response to a person's looks is instinctual, part of our biological makeup. But that shouldn't stop us checking our prejudices and rejecting them, especially when it comes to who we want to run the country. So let's kick uglism out of politics. We need to protect ourselves from ignoring the best candidate because he or she looks a bit "other". Maybe we need positive discrimination and all-ugly shortlists, or we could make the veil compulsory for all politicians. Maybe the electoral reform we need isn't AV or PR, but changing our voting system so it's like The Voice, with voters unable to see candidates till they've cast their votes by hitting a red button on a swivel chair. And yes, installing 40m swivel chairs nationwide could be costly, but who are we to put a price on democracy? It's the only way. Otherwise we'll just end up choosing our leader according to who looks best in a swimsuit, and I for one don't fancy Miliband's chances in Speedos.

results.webTitle: MPs debate the bedroom tax: Politics live blog

fields.headline: MPs debate the bedroom tax: Politics live blog
fields.trailText: Andrew Sparrow's rolling coverage of the day's political developments
fields.bodyText: • Several Lib Dem MPs have indicated that they will rebel when the Commons votes tonight on a Labour motion calling for the abolition of the bedroom tax. Tim Farron, the Lib Dem president, is planning to vote against his government and, in the chamber, Charles Kennedy, the former Lib Dem leader, and Andrew George have both said they are opposed to the bedroom tax. Another four Lib Dems have signed a Commons motion strongly criticising the bedroom tax. Party insiders believe that up to 15 Lib Dems could rebel when the votes comes at 7pm. • Jeremy Hunt, the health secretary, has been accused of "running scared" after health department officials briefed newspapers and not parliament on a report due to be published on Wednesday on plans for a total overhaul of the NHS accident and emergency departments. • Ed Davey, the energy secretary, has accused the big six energy companies of using customers as "cash cows" by raising bills unfairly. As Terry Macalister reports, he also renewed his attack on Ed Miliband, describing the Labour leader's proposal to freeze energy prices as "intellectually bankrupt". • Michael Gove, the education secretary, has said that more struggling children's services departments in England could be taken over by independent providers. You can read the full text of his speech here. • Kathryn Hudson, the parliamentary commissioner for standards, has decided that there is insufficient evidence to justify an inquiry into Mark Pritchard, the Conservative MP accused by the Daily Telegraph of offering to exploit foreign contacts to set up business deals. • Theresa May, the home secretary, plans to strip British terror suspects of their citizenship and take away their UK passports even if it leaves them stateless, Whitehall sources have confirmed. • The government has unveiled plans to stop legal firms bombarding people with unsolicited phone calls and texts in order to find customers for compensation claim cases. That's all from me for today. Thanks for the comments. Here's a politics afternoon reading list. • John McDermott at Financial Times says Sir John Major is right about the influence of private schools, but that it is not clear what he wants to change. Whether the “upper echelons of power” are slightly more or slightly less closed than a generation ago, Sir John’s comments hint at an important truth: Britain is a deeply unfair meritocracy. This sounds contradictory but as the great historian David Kynaston has written, private schools are “those highly resourced, highly skilled and ferociously dedicated blockers of downward mobility”. They perpetuate networks of privilege but they are presumably also fantastic schools. This combination of positional and actual good helps explain why their fees have gone up so quickly in the two decades – and why many families scrimp and save to send their kids there ... It is less than clear what Sir John is recommending be done. Perhaps he just wants the Tory leadership to come across as less posh than they are. He dutifully mentions education, which is of course important, especially in the early years. But if what he genuinely wants is an end to private schools’ dominance of British life he would surely need to engage with what Prof Kynaston says: that unless something is done to stop them being so exclusive and so good at what they do, then “the current political consensus is … almost meaningless.” • Lord Ashcroft at ConservativeHome says he can understand why Ed Miliband wants leaders' debate before the election, but that they could backfire. The risk for Mr Miliband is that far from giving him a chance to explain why he thinks price controls are workable, TV debates would further expose the idea’s superficiality. As President Bartlet observed in the West Wing, the ten-word answer can be deadly in politics, but where are the next ten words, and the ten after that? And as Henrique Capriles, the Venezuelan opposition leader, said of President Maduro, “every time he opens his mouth, he scares away the investments that create employment and worsens the crisis”. Rather than transforming perceptions of Miliband after five years as Labour leader, TV debates could confirm them. Meanwhile, they would allow Cameron and Clegg to argue at greater length that higher living standards are achieved by proper economic management, not least controlling the deficit, and by encouraging businesses rather than threatening them. These are risks, not certainties. A gamble on TV debates may yet help Labour, if the Tories can be persuaded to take part despite having more to lose. But Miliband’s eagerness to hold them is not a signal of confidence. It’s the opposite. • Grant Shapps has used a Buzzfeed list to attack Labour. He has produced a Buzzfeed posted entitled: 12 Facts - Why Energy Bills Are Sky High And What We Could Do About It. • Philip Cowley and Mark Stuart on their Revolts blog list the 10 most interesting votes in the Commons since 2010. The Hansard for the opening of the bedroom tax debate is now on the Hansard website. In the Commons the Tory MP David TC Davies defended the government's bedroom tax. He even suggested that the welfare cuts did not go far enough. He made the point when he told an anecdote about doing some filming with the BBC with people affected by the policy. I met a young couple, a teenage couple, 17 years old, neither of whom had ever worked in their lives, they were in a two or a one-bedroom flat and were being expected to suffer some inconvenience, perhaps move into a studio flat. And again I say to the ministers on my front-bench, you are being far too generous in many instances. Why should the state be paying for two people to set up in, frankly, a teenage love nest? When I was 17 years old, if I wanted to see my girlfriend at the age of 17, I would go and see her on a park bench in Newport. Why is the government paying for them to have a flat all by themselves at all, never-mind whether it's one-bedroom or two-bedroom? And I got into a lot a of trouble because I suggested to the young man that perhaps he should go out and find a job himself. And he said there weren't any... I said, 'Well why don't you move to where the work is' and I immediately ran into a whole load of criticism for that. I even had someone email me here. He said, 'You're a Christian, you should be serving the Lord, one day you will stand by the Lord and account for this hardship'. And I wrote back and said, 'I read my bible, I don't see anywhere in the bible where it says 17 year olds should be given a flat but I see plenty of examples of people who have had to move for a better way of life, whether it was Abraham going off to the Promised Land or Moses or the disciples who toured all over Europe'. They all moved. I've taken the quote from the Press Association. Four Lib Dem MPs have tabled a Commons early day motion saying that the bedroom tax is a mistake and that, unless there are extensive changes - including a wider range of exemptions, and the removal of penalties from people who do not have the option of moving to a smaller home - it should be abolished. You can read the text of the motion here. Broadly it reflects the motion passed at the Lib Dem conference in September. The four Lib Dem MPs are: Greg Mulholland, Ian Swales, Adrian Sanders and Roger Williams. Caroline Lucas, the Green MP, used an intervention at the start of the bedroom tax debate to condemn the policy. She has sent out a news release saying that 1,270 people in Brighton and Hove are affected by the policy, of whom 300 are now behind with their rent. Of those 300 people, 205 are disabled. The Green-led council in Brighton and Hove has a policy of not evicting people who fall behind with their rent because of the bedroom tax, she says. This is a brutal policy, and the government simply doesn’t care that some of the most vulnerable in our society – many of whom have disabilities – are being hit hard. It’s worse than Victorian that the poor are being targeted by this tax, especially when the richest are benefiting from tax cuts. And it’s horrifying that such a high proportion of those affected are disabled. The policy risks pushing people who are already desperate into the hands of pay day loan sharks. Webb, though, was good. But ultimately he was on a hopeless mission. Reeves hammered away at the injustices and inconsistencies of the bedroom with brisk, machine-gun efficiency, but it was the Labour interventions, and the stories they contained, that really sunk the government. This is from the Press Association. House prices surged at their fastest annual rate in almost three years in September and remained close to an all-time high recorded the previous month, official figures have shown. Prices rose by 3.8% year-on-year to reach £245,000 on average, marking a slight decrease on the peak of £246,000 recorded in August, according to the Office for National Statistics (ONS). The annual uplift is the highest seen since October 2010 and comes amid calls for the Government to ease the upward pressure on house prices being fuelled by its new Help to Buy scheme by building more homes to meet demand. The figures were released as the Royal Institution of Chartered Surveyors (Rics) called for urgent action to address the supply of homes, which it said is "nowhere near" that which is needed to cope with "soaring" demand from buyers. Rics said the number of surveyors reporting house prices lifting across the country has surged to an 11-year high as the Government's new Help to Buy scheme fuels "soaring" demand from buyers. The ONS said that on a seasonally-adjusted basis, UK house prices were unchanged between August and September. House prices in London have soared by 9.4% over the year to reach £434,000 typically. The year-on-year increase reflected growth of 4.2% in England, where typical prices reached £255,000, and 1.4% in Wales, where prices were £163,000 on average. Prices in England have fallen slightly from record levels seen the previous month but are still 0.8% higher than their previous 2008 peak, the ONS said. The rises were offset by annual falls of 1.1% in Scotland, where typical prices fell to £181,000 and 1.5% in Northern Ireland, where the average price edged down to £127,000. Prices in Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland are still well below the peaks seen in the property boom. Northern Ireland has seen the sharpest decline and prices are still at 50% below their previous peak. Here's Catherine McKinnell, the shadow Treasury minister, on the inflation figures. (See 9.37am.) This fall in the inflation rate is welcome, but with prices still rising much faster than wages the cost-of-living crisis under David Cameron continues. And with inflation-busting hikes in energy prices in the pipeline families and pensioners will carry on feeling the squeeze. Here's Hilary Osborne's Guardian story about the EDF price rise. (See 9.27am.) EDF says it is increasing prices by less than other firms because it is assuming that the government will take some green levies out of bills. If the government does not do that, prices could rise further, it says. Here's an extract from Hilary's story. EDF said it had taken action ahead of the outcome of the government's review of the costs of the ECO scheme to help vulnerable households and other green schemes, designed to bring down costs for consumers. It said if the government makes bigger changes to the costs of its social and environmental schemes than it has anticipated, it would pass these savings on to customers, but if the changes were smaller there could be further price rises. Angela Knight, the head of Energy UK, has been speaking to the BBC from the organisation's conference in London. Here are the main points. • Knight said the industry "absolutely understands the concerns that are being raised by their customers". • She said energy firms were not to blame for prices going up. Overwhelmingly the reason why prices are going up are not because the energy company has got a real contribution in that price increase. It’s pass-throughs onto the bills – the policies that come through because of the government policies and because of the need to invest in networks and in distributions. • She rejected a suggestion that the energy companies wanted green levies to be scrapped. What they wanted was a debate over whether those measures should be funded through bills, or whether they should be funded by central government, she said. I've taken the quote from PoliticsHome. And here is some Twitter coverage of her speech to the conference. There are two polls around today. For the record, here are the figures. ICM for the Guardian Labour: 38% (no change from ICM last month) Conservatives: 30% (down 4) Lib Dems: 13% (up 1) Ukip: 10% (up 2) Labour lead: 8 points (up 4) According to Electoral Calculus, this would give Labour a majority of 96. YouGov for the Sun Labour: 40% (up 1 from YouGov in the Sunday Times) Conservatives: 33% (down 1) Ukip: 11% (no change) Lib Dems: 9% (down 1) Labour lead: 7 points (up 2) Government approval: -31 (down 1) According to Electoral Calculus, this would give Labour a majority of 88. Here are the headline inflation figures. • The rate of consumer price index (CPI) inflation fell to 2.2% in October - its lowest level in more than a year - from 2.7% in September. • The headline rate of retail price index (RPI) inflation fell to 2.6% from 3.2% in September. The Office for National Statistics news release about the figures is here. Here's the summary explanation for the fall. The largest contributions to the fall in the rate came from the transport (notably motor fuels) and education (tuition fees) sectors. And here's the statistical bulletin with full details (pdf). The Press Association has just snapped this. EDF Energy said today that average gas and electricity bills will rise by 3.9% from January 3. Here's the extract from Ed Davey's speech to the Energy UK conference that was released in advance overnight. We need our energy companies to be profitable so they can invest in our energy infrastructure, secure the energy supplies of the future, develop more energy efficient technologies, and create jobs. But those profits cannot come at the expense of the elderly, the vulnerable, and the poorest in our society. Customers are not just cash cows to be squeezed in the pursuit of a higher return for shareholders. Trust between those who supply energy and those who use it is breaking down. It is so difficult for people to work out what exactly they are paying for that they fear the big energy companies are taking them for a ride when bills go up. Fair or not, they look at the big suppliers and they see a reflection of the greed that consumed the banks. So this is a ‘fred the shred’ moment for the industry. You deliver an essential public service, so your industry must serve the public – and the public must have trust in what you do. So we have opened up our books and we are looking at how we can reduce the impact of government policies on bills. But our commitment must be matched by a commitment in industry to open up your books and set out exactly how you are bearing down on your own costs to make bills as low as possible. The industry must be much more transparent and Ofgem will have our full support to introduce whatever regulations are necessary to deliver that greater transparency. Comparing the energy firms to the banks may not go down particularly well, but at least Angela Knight, Energy UK's chief executive, will feel at home. In her last job she was chief executive at the British Bankers' Association, the trade body for banks. (Don't be surprised if her next job involves defending payday lenders." Energy UK has already hit back at Davey. This is what a spokesman has been saying: The energy industry is already working hard to ensure everyone can keep the lights on and stay warm this winter. The best way to do this is for everyone to work together, which is why this tit-for-tat Punch and Judy show of insults is so unproductive. The energy industry is vital to the UK. It is a major employer, a serious investor and a significant taxpayer. As analysis from UBS shows, about 95% of rising energy costs are out of the hands of the energy companies and can be attributed to government policies and other network, social and environmental costs. Here's the timetable for the conference. Davey will be speaking at 11.50am, not 9.30am as I thought earlier. It's busy today, but without any one political story dominating the news. I will concentrate particularly on the Ed Davey speech, and the opening of the Commons debate on the bedroom tax, but there is plenty of other politics to keep an eye on too. Here's the agenda for the day. 9.30am: Inflation figures are released. 10.15am: The Probation Chiefs Association and the Probation Association give evidence to the Commons justice committee. 11am: Ed Miliband gives a speech announcing a Labour taskforce looking at digital skills. As Patrick Wintour reports, Miliband will say that it will be headed by the former Tomorrow's World presenter Maggie Philbin. 11.15am: Justine Greening, the international development secretary, gives evidence to the Commons international development committee about the development implications of Scottish independence. 11.30am: Michael Gove, the education secretary, gives a speech on children's services. As Randeep Ramesh reports, Gove will announce a "personal" mission for a radical plan to reform social work on Monday – citing his own life which was "transformed because of the skill of social workers and the love of parents who were not my biological mother and father" as proof of the profession's power to change lives. 11.50am: Ed Davey, the energy secretary, speaks at the Energy UK annual conference. As Damian Carrington reports, Davey will say that energy companies are facing are facing a "Fred the Shred" moment because the public sees in them "a reflection of the greed that consumed the banks". 12.30pm: MPs begin a debate on a Labour motion calling for the repeal of the bedroom tax. Rachel Reeves, the shadow work and pensions secretary, will open for Labour, but Iain Duncan Smith, the work and pensions secretary, will not be responding. Instead it will be another minister. 2.30pm: Norman Lamb, the care minister, gives evidence to the Commons health committee on managing long-term care conditions. 2.45pm: David Anderson QC, the independent reviewer of terrorist legislation, gives evidence to the Commons home affairs committee. My colleague Paul Owen will be covering the hearing on his NSA files live blog. As usual, I’ll also be covering all the breaking political news as well as looking at the papers and bringing you the best politics from the web. I’ll post a lunchtime summary before 12.30pm and another in the afternoon. If you want to follow me on Twitter, I’m on @AndrewSparrow.

results.webTitle: A hostile environment: Immigration and British politics | Editorial

fields.headline: A hostile environment: Immigration and British politics
fields.trailText: Editorial: Immigration was until recently running at historic highs, and any politician needs to face up to public unease
fields.bodyText: There is a hard business case for immigration, and if you don't want to take it from meticulous empirical economists, take it from America's mainstream right. "Immigrants come with new skills and new ideas. They fill a critical part in our labour market," said George W Bush. "They work hard for a better life." George Osborne's fast-track Chinese visas reflect this, but Britain's right more often relegates financial concerns below the emotive dimension. The "immigration bill for hard-working people", which Theresa May published last week, is a ragbag of measures to make outlaws of individuals striving for a better life. Immigration was until recently running at historic highs, and any politician needs to face up to public unease. The polling, however, does not point to unremitting hostility, but rather a mood of fluidity, even confusion. Voters rank immigration highly among the issues "facing the country", but only as a marginal concern in their own lives. They judge it to be bad for the economy, and yet are inclined to believe that individual migrants work harder than average. The perceived difficulties bear no relation to the number of newcomers within each region, and are everywhere imagined to bite more at the national than the local level. It is almost as if everybody believes that immigration is someone else's problem. With experience counting for less than impressions, the power of suggestion is enhanced. The insinuations in the home secretary's bill are alarming; the spin centred on making immigrants pay for the NHS, a goal at once superfluous (immigrants can already be billed for non-emergency care) and beside the point (immigrants are healthier than most). The actual proposal here is for Mrs May to assume sweeping enabling powers to charge people wishing to come or to stay as much as she likes, for any reason. The legislation also seeks to tie the hands of judges weighing immigrants' right to a family life, a dubious restriction which could fall foul of Strasbourg, if it does not first create a clash in the British courts. Traditional conservatives should be horrified by a wheeze designed to short-circuit the constitutional wiring, but a crisis would further Mrs May's dream of turning back the clock on two-thirds of a century of civilisation by quitting the European convention. The real heart of the bill is the abolition of most appeals. Few bureaucratic decisions have the power to break families or fortunes in the manner of the choice to admit or expel from these shores; and yet – as the supreme court has noted – this is a field in which official process is marked by box-ticking "rigidity". Without appeals, more bad decisions will go unchallenged. One perverse consequence will be an increase in costly judicial reviews, the final buck stop that cannot be scrapped, which Whitehall has spent years trying to restrict. During the long, dragging process of such reviews, a second perversity will be to criminalise applicants who are legal today by virtue of an active appeal. This is part of a broader agenda which also includes barring irregular migrants from bank accounts and driving licences. Cash-in-hand work and uninsured driving will increase, with attendant effect on the public purse and public safety. Mrs May has obviously decided this price is worth paying for her overriding stated aim of making Britain "a hostile environment" for outsiders. The lure of the rich world to the poor is such that migrants' hopes are not easily deterred, as dreadful tales of watery graves in the Mediterranean underline. Many immigrants will not go home, but live here hidden, hopeless, and beyond the reach of the law; that will make British communities a more hostile environment for natives and newcomers alike. For Labour, which has a mixed record in this area, Yvette Cooper pledges to stand firm against "National Front" language. The Lib Dems have kicked back against fines for landlords housing irregular migrants, but now need to widen their attack. As it stands, this is a bill to stir searching Lib Dem questions about exactly what sort of government they are in.

fields.headline: Politics live: readers' edition - Friday 11 October
fields.trailText: Share breaking news, leave links to interesting articles online and chat about the day's political events in our open thread
fields.bodyText: I'm not writing my usual Politics Live blog today, but, as an alternative, here's Politics Live: readers' edition. It's intended to be a place where you can catch up with the latest news and find links to good politics blogs and articles on the web. Please feel free to use this as somewhere you can comment on any of the day's political stories - just as you do when I'm writing the daily blog.
